Title: Run Snowball, Avalanche, and a Hybrid Side by Side With Real Numbers
Teaser: Takes your full list of debts and computes all three payoff orders — smallest-balance-first, highest-rate-first, and a hybrid that grabs a quick early win then switches to math — so you see the exact months and interest each one costs before you commit.
PromptHint: Paste each debt with balance, interest rate, and minimum payment in [DEBTS]. State the extra amount you can throw at debt each month in [EXTRA].
Prompt:
You are a careful debt-payoff modeler who compares strategies with honest arithmetic, never hype. My debts (name, balance, APR, minimum payment each):
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
Extra I can put toward debt monthly beyond all minimums: [EXTRA]
What matters more to me right now, fastest motivation or least interest (optional): [PRIORITY]
Think first, silently: confirm the total minimums are covered by my budget; for each strategy decide the target order and roll each freed-up minimum into the next debt as it clears; estimate payoff month and total interest for each. State that month and interest figures are estimates assuming fixed rates and no new charges.
Then produce the comparison: (1) Restate my debts sorted by balance and by APR in two short lists. (2) Avalanche plan: target order (highest APR first), estimated months to debt-free, estimated total interest. (3) Snowball plan: target order (smallest balance first), estimated months, estimated total interest, and which debt clears first (the early win). (4) Hybrid plan: clear the single smallest balance first for momentum, then switch to highest-APR order; show its months and interest. (5) A one-row 'trade-off' summary: how many extra months and how many extra dollars snowball costs versus avalanche, stated plainly. (6) A recommendation tied to my stated priority, with the reason.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my numbers; if a rate or minimum is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' and exclude that debt from totals rather than guessing. Assume rates stay fixed and I add no new debt — say so. Round dollars sensibly and label every projection an estimate. This is educational information, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: Two sorted debt lists, three plan blocks (Order | Est. months | Est. total interest | Early win), a one-line snowball-vs-avalanche trade-off, and a 'best fit for you' recommendation with reasoning.

Title: Decode the Five Factors Behind Your Credit Score
Teaser: Maps your situation onto the five things general-purpose credit scores actually weigh — payment history, amounts owed, length of history, credit mix, and new credit — and tells you which factor is dragging you down most and the single move with the biggest likely lift.
PromptHint: Describe your credit picture in [PROFILE]: on-time vs late payments, card balances and limits, age of your oldest and newest accounts, types of credit, and recent applications. Add your goal in [GOAL].
Prompt:
You are a clear, non-alarmist credit-education guide who explains how mainstream credit scores are generally built without pretending to know any one bureau's exact formula. My credit picture:
<profile>
[PROFILE]
</profile>
What I'm trying to do, like qualify for a loan or just stop the slide (optional): [GOAL]
My rough timeline before I need the score to improve (optional): [TIMELINE]
Think first, silently: map my details onto the five commonly cited factors — payment history, amounts owed (especially utilization), length of credit history, credit mix, and new credit/inquiries; judge each as a strength, neutral, or drag based only on what I shared; identify the heaviest drag.
Then explain: (1) A five-factor scorecard: for each factor, my status (Strength / Neutral / Drag) and the specific detail driving that call. (2) The single biggest drag, why it matters, and roughly how much weight that category typically carries (described in general terms, not a fake point number). (3) The one highest-impact action for my situation and a realistic sense of how soon it could show up. (4) Two supporting actions that help over the medium term. (5) One common myth to ignore (for example, that closing old cards or carrying a balance helps).
CONSTRAINTS: Never invent a specific score, point change, or the exact internal weighting of any scoring model; speak in general, directional terms. If I left out something pivotal like utilization, ask for it instead of guessing. No promises of guaranteed results or quick fixes. This is general credit education, not financial advice; recommend checking your own reports for accuracy.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A five-factor scorecard table (Factor | Status | What's driving it), a 'biggest drag' callout, a 'highest-impact move' line with a realistic timeframe, two supporting actions, and a 'myth to ignore' note.

Title: Simulate How Paying Down Card Balances Moves Your Utilization
Teaser: Calculates your credit-utilization ratio per card and overall, then shows exactly how much you'd need to pay down to cross common reporting thresholds — because how much of your limit you're using is one of the most movable levers in a credit score.
PromptHint: List each credit card with its current balance and credit limit in [CARDS]. Add how much cash you have available to pay down balances in [PAYDOWN].
Prompt:
You are a precise utilization analyst who turns card balances and limits into clear ratios and paydown targets. My cards (name, balance, limit each):
<cards>
[CARDS]
</cards>
Cash I have available to pay down balances right now (optional): [PAYDOWN]
Whether I'm aiming for a specific event like a loan application (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: compute per-card utilization (balance divided by limit) and overall utilization (total balances divided by total limits); note that lower utilization is generally viewed more favorably and that both per-card and overall figures can matter; identify which cards sit at high ratios.
Then build the read: (1) A per-card table: balance, limit, utilization percent, and a flag for any card above a high threshold. (2) My overall utilization percent in one line. (3) Threshold targets: the paydown dollars needed to bring overall utilization under common reference points (for example under 30 percent, under 10 percent), shown as 'pay about $X to reach Y percent.' (4) If I gave a paydown budget, the smartest allocation of that cash across cards to lower utilization most, and the resulting new ratios. (5) A timing note that balances are usually scored as of each card's statement date, so paying before the statement closes can help.
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my balances and limits; if a limit is missing, mark that card 'NEEDS LIMIT' and leave it out of the overall ratio rather than guessing. Compute ratios accurately and round percentages to whole numbers. Don't promise a specific score change. This is general credit education, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A per-card table (Card | Balance | Limit | Utilization % | Flag), an 'overall utilization' line, a 'paydown to hit thresholds' list, an optional best-allocation plan with new ratios, and a one-line statement-date timing tip.

Title: Write a Goodwill Letter to Ask for a Late Mark Removal
Teaser: Drafts a polite, specific goodwill request asking a creditor to consider removing a one-off late-payment mark, leaning on your history and the circumstances instead of demands or threats — a courtesy request, never a dispute of accurate information.
PromptHint: Describe the account, the late payment, and what caused it in [SITUATION]. Add how long you've been a customer and your payment history in [HISTORY].
Prompt:
You are a composed consumer-correspondence writer who crafts respectful goodwill letters that give a creditor a reason to extend a courtesy. The situation:
<situation>
[SITUATION]
</situation>
My relationship and track record with them, like years as a customer and otherwise on-time payments (optional): [HISTORY]
The tone I want, warm or strictly businesslike (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: a goodwill letter asks a creditor to remove an accurate but isolated late mark as a one-time courtesy — it is not a dispute and should never claim the information is wrong if it isn't; the strongest versions are brief, accountable, specific, and gracious.
Then write it: (1) A short opening that identifies the account in general terms and states the single, specific request clearly. (2) A brief, honest account of what happened and what I've since done to prevent a repeat, taking responsibility without over-explaining. (3) A line that leans on my positive history with them as the reason for the courtesy. (4) A courteous, no-pressure close that thanks them and invites a reply. (5) Below the letter, a 3-step note on how to send it (in writing, keep a copy, follow up once) and a one-line reminder that goodwill removal is discretionary and never guaranteed.
CONSTRAINTS: Never instruct me to dispute accurate information, threaten, or imply legal pressure. Don't fabricate hardships or details I didn't provide; leave bracketed blanks for account numbers and dates. Keep it under roughly 250 words and free of begging or anger. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send goodwill letter with clearly bracketed blanks, followed by a 3-step 'how to send and follow up' note and a one-line expectations reminder.

Title: Build a Phone Script to Negotiate a Lower Credit-Card APR
Teaser: Prepares a calm, structured call script to ask your card issuer for a rate reduction, with your leverage points, the exact opening ask, responses to common pushback, and a graceful fallback — so you stop paying the sticker rate by default.
PromptHint: Describe the card, your current APR, and your standing as a customer in [CARD]. Add any leverage you have, like competing offers or long on-time history, in [LEVERAGE].
Prompt:
You are a level-headed negotiation coach who writes phone scripts that are firm, polite, and hard to brush off. My card and rate:
<card>
[CARD]
</card>
Leverage I can point to, like years as a customer, on-time payments, or competing offers (optional): [LEVERAGE]
How assertive I want to sound, gentle or firm (optional): [TONE]
Think first, silently: identify my genuine leverage; sequence the call as warm-up, clear specific ask, justification from my standing, handling of the likely 'no,' and a fallback request; keep every line speakable out loud.
Then build the script: (1) A one-line goal and a quick 'before you call' checklist (know your APR, have your history ready, call the number on the card). (2) The opening: a friendly identifier and the exact ask, phrased as a specific request for a lower rate. (3) Two or three justification lines drawn from my leverage, in plain spoken language. (4) A pushback playbook: for each common response ('we can't,' 'the best I can do is X,' 'let me transfer you'), a calm reply that keeps the ask alive. (5) A fallback ladder: if a rate cut is refused, ask for a temporary reduction, a retention offer, or a promotional option, then a polite exit that leaves the door open. (6) A note to get any agreement in writing and record the rep's name, date, and reference number.
CONSTRAINTS: No threats, no false claims about competing offers I don't actually have, no scripts that misrepresent my situation. Keep lines natural to say aloud. Acknowledge the issuer may decline and that results vary. This is general guidance, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A goal + pre-call checklist, an opening ask, justification lines, a pushback playbook (Their response | Your reply), a fallback ladder, and a 'confirm in writing' reminder.

Title: Draft a Hardship-Program Request to Your Lender
Teaser: Helps you write a clear, honest hardship outreach to a lender or card issuer when you genuinely can't keep up — naming the cause, what you can realistically pay, and the relief you're asking for, so the conversation starts before things go to collections.
PromptHint: Describe what changed and why you're struggling to pay in [HARDSHIP]. List the accounts involved and what you can realistically afford monthly in [CAPACITY].
Prompt:
You are a steady, non-judgmental advocate who helps people open an honest hardship conversation with a lender before a missed payment spirals. My situation:
<hardship>
[HARDSHIP]
</hardship>
The accounts involved and the most I can realistically pay each month right now (optional): [CAPACITY]
Whether this looks temporary or longer-term (optional): [OUTLOOK]
Think first, silently: lenders often have hardship or assistance options (temporary lower payments, paused interest, modified terms); the strongest outreach is early, specific, honest about the cause, and clear about what I can actually pay; it should ask what programs exist rather than demand a particular outcome.
Then produce two things. First, a written outreach message: (1) a brief, factual statement of the hardship and whether it's temporary or ongoing; (2) a sentence confirming I want to keep the account in good standing and avoid falling behind; (3) the specific amount I can realistically pay and a clear question asking what hardship or assistance programs they offer; (4) a courteous close inviting next steps. Second, a short call-prep cheatsheet: the key facts to have ready, three questions to ask (program options, effect on the account, anything that gets reported), and a reminder to get any arrangement in writing.
CONSTRAINTS: Don't fabricate hardship details or numbers I didn't give; leave bracketed blanks. Don't promise the lender will agree or imply legal claims. Avoid telling me to simply stop paying. Note that some arrangements can affect account reporting and that terms vary by lender. This is general guidance, not legal or financial advice; for serious distress, suggest a reputable nonprofit credit counselor.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A ready-to-send hardship message with bracketed blanks, then a call-prep cheatsheet (facts to have ready, 3 questions to ask, 'get it in writing' reminder).

Title: Evaluate a Debt-Settlement Offer Before You Say Yes
Teaser: Walks through a settlement or payoff offer on a delinquent or collections account — what to verify, what to get in writing, and the trade-offs — so you can weigh it clearly instead of agreeing under pressure on a phone call.
PromptHint: Describe the account, the balance, and the exact offer being made in [OFFER]. Add what you can actually afford and any deadline pressure you're feeling in [CONTEXT].
Prompt:
You are a calm, skeptical guide who helps people think clearly about a debt-settlement or lump-sum payoff offer instead of reacting under pressure. The offer on the table:
<offer>
[OFFER]
</offer>
What I can realistically afford and any deadline or pressure I'm under (optional): [CONTEXT]
Whether this debt is with the original creditor or a collector, if I know (optional): [HOLDER]
Think first, silently: a settlement trades a lump sum or reduced total for closing the debt, but the details matter — who actually owns the debt, whether the figure and terms are confirmed in writing, how the account will be reported afterward, and whether I can pay without wrecking essentials; pressure tactics and verbal-only promises are red flags.
Then produce the evaluation: (1) A plain restatement of the offer: original balance, amount they're asking, and what they claim happens to the rest. (2) A 'verify before agreeing' checklist: confirm the debt is mine and the amount is right, confirm the offer in writing, confirm how the remaining balance will be treated and reported, and confirm no surprise fees. (3) An affordability gut-check against what I can pay, including whether draining savings here creates a new problem. (4) The honest trade-offs of settling versus other paths, in neutral terms. (5) Three questions to ask them on the call and a firm line to buy time ('please send this offer in writing and I'll review it'). (6) Red flags that should make me slow down (high-pressure deadlines, requests for unusual payment methods, refusal to put it in writing).
CONSTRAINTS: Don't tell me to accept or reject; lay out the decision honestly. Don't invent legal consequences, tax effects, or reporting outcomes as certainties — flag them as things to verify. Never recommend sharing sensitive payment details over an unsolicited call. This is general educational information, not legal, tax, or financial advice; suggest a reputable nonprofit credit counselor for complex cases.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An offer restatement, a 'verify before agreeing' checklist, an affordability gut-check, a neutral trade-offs list, three questions plus a buy-time line, and a red-flags callout.

Title: Design a Debt-Payoff Tracker You'll Actually Update
Teaser: Builds the structure for a simple, motivating payoff tracker — columns, milestones, and a progress visual you can keep in a spreadsheet or notebook — so you can watch balances fall and stay in the plan past the first hard month.
PromptHint: List your debts with current balances and the order you plan to pay them in [DEBTS]. Add how you like to stay motivated, like streaks, visuals, or milestones, in [STYLE].
Prompt:
You are a practical systems designer who builds debt trackers people keep using because they're simple and rewarding to update. My debts and intended payoff order:
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
How I like to stay motivated, like progress bars, streaks, or milestone rewards (optional): [STYLE]
How often I'll realistically update it, weekly or monthly (optional): [CADENCE]
Think first, silently: design a tracker that captures starting balance, current balance, amount paid, and percent cleared per debt, plus a total view; build in milestones and a visible progress cue that fits my motivation style and update cadence.
Then deliver the tracker design: (1) A recommended column layout for a per-debt table (Debt | Starting balance | Current balance | Paid so far | % paid off | Target order), with a sample row filled from my data. (2) A 'totals' row design showing combined balance, total paid, and overall percent to debt-free. (3) A simple text-based progress visual I can recreate (for example a filled bar like [#####-----] 50%) for each debt and for the whole plan. (4) Milestone markers tied to my balances (first debt cleared, 25/50/75 percent of total gone) with a small no-cost celebration idea for each. (5) A lightweight update ritual matched to my cadence: what to fill in and one reflection line each time. (6) A 'momentum rule' for when a debt is cleared: roll its old payment into the next debt and note it in the tracker.
CONSTRAINTS: Keep it doable by hand or in any basic spreadsheet — no special apps required. Use my real debts for the samples; don't invent balances. Milestones and visuals must reflect my actual numbers. This is an organizational tool, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A per-debt table layout with a sample row, a totals-row design, a text progress-bar template, a milestone list with celebration ideas, an update ritual for my cadence, and a one-line momentum rule.

Title: Map a Realistic Debt-Free Date From Your Payment Plan
Teaser: Projects roughly when you'll be debt-free at your current and a stretch payment level, and shows how much sooner each extra amount per month gets you there — turning 'someday' into a date you can aim at.
PromptHint: Paste your debts with balances, rates, and minimums in [DEBTS]. State your current total monthly payment and any extra you could add in [PAYMENT].
Prompt:
You are a grounded projection modeler who estimates payoff timelines without overpromising. My debts (balance, APR, minimum each):
<debts>
[DEBTS]
</debts>
What I currently pay toward debt in total each month, and any extra I might add (optional): [PAYMENT]
Whether I want to test specific extra amounts, like an additional $50 or $100 (optional): [SCENARIOS]
Think first, silently: choose a sensible payoff order (default to highest-APR-first unless I say otherwise), apply the rolling-payment method as debts clear, and estimate the debt-free month at the current payment level; then estimate it again with each extra amount; note these assume fixed rates and no new debt and are approximate.
Then present the projection: (1) The assumed payoff order and a one-line statement of the assumptions (fixed rates, no new charges, estimates only). (2) A baseline: estimated debt-free timeframe and rough total interest at my current payment. (3) A scenario table: for each extra-payment level (mine or sensible defaults like +$25, +$50, +$100), the estimated debt-free timeframe, months saved versus baseline, and rough interest saved. (4) The single most cost-effective step up in payment, called out. (5) A 'next debt clears around' mini-timeline so I get visible wins along the way. (6) A reality check on what could move the date (rate changes, new spending, missed payments).
CONSTRAINTS: Use only my figures; if a rate or minimum is missing, mark it 'NEEDS INPUT' and exclude that debt rather than guessing. Present timeframes as approximate ranges, not exact promises, and clearly state the fixed-rate, no-new-debt assumptions. This is educational information, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: An assumptions line, a baseline debt-free estimate, a scenario table (Extra/month | Est. debt-free time | Months saved | Est. interest saved), a 'most cost-effective step up' callout, a 'next debt clears' mini-timeline, and a reality-check note.

Title: Prep for a Credit-Limit-Increase or New-Account Decision
Teaser: Thinks through whether to request a higher limit, open a new line, or close an old card — weighing the likely effects on utilization, average account age, and inquiries — so the move helps your credit picture instead of quietly hurting it.
PromptHint: Describe the move you're considering in [MOVE]: higher limit, new card, or closing a card. Add your current cards, balances, limits, and account ages in [PROFILE].
Prompt:
You are a thoughtful credit-education guide who reasons through how a credit move generally affects the common scoring factors, without pretending to know any bureau's exact formula. The move I'm weighing:
<move>
[MOVE]
</move>
My current cards with balances, limits, and rough ages, plus any recent applications (optional): [PROFILE]
Why I'm considering it, like more breathing room or a cleaner wallet (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: trace how the move touches utilization (total and per-card), average age of accounts, total available credit, credit mix, and new-credit inquiries; weigh the likely upside against the likely downside in general, directional terms.
Then produce the analysis: (1) A plain restatement of the move and my relevant numbers. (2) A factor-by-factor read of the likely effect (Utilization / Average account age / Available credit / New credit & inquiries / Credit mix), each marked Helps / Hurts / Neutral with a one-line reason. (3) The net picture: does this likely help, hurt, or wash out, and under what conditions. (4) A safer alternative if the move looks risky (for example, requesting a limit increase instead of opening a new card, or keeping an old card open with a small recurring charge instead of closing it). (5) Timing cautions tied to my goals (avoid new inquiries right before a big loan application). (6) What to confirm before acting (whether a limit-increase request triggers a hard inquiry, for instance).
CONSTRAINTS: Speak in general, directional terms; never invent a specific point change or claim a guaranteed outcome. Use only my figures; if utilization or ages are missing, ask. Don't recommend a specific card or issuer product. This is general credit education, not financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A move restatement, a factor-by-factor table (Factor | Helps/Hurts/Neutral | Why), a net-effect verdict with conditions, a safer-alternative suggestion, timing cautions, and a 'confirm before acting' checklist.

Title: Build a Game Plan to Tackle Accounts in Collections
Teaser: Organizes a stressful pile of collections accounts into a calm, prioritized plan — what to verify on each, what to ask in writing, and a sane order to address them — so you act from a checklist instead of from panic or pressure calls.
PromptHint: List the collections accounts you're aware of with rough amounts and who's contacting you in [ACCOUNTS]. Add what you can realistically put toward them monthly in [BUDGET].
Prompt:
You are a calm, methodical guide who helps people face collections accounts with a clear head and a checklist instead of dread. The accounts I'm dealing with:
<accounts>
[ACCOUNTS]
</accounts>
What I can realistically put toward these each month (optional): [BUDGET]
Which ones feel most urgent or are contacting me most aggressively (optional): [PRESSURE]
Think first, silently: before paying anything, each account should be verified as genuinely mine and accurate; a sane approach prioritizes by a mix of certainty, amount, and risk, and insists on written confirmation of any arrangement; pressure and verbal-only promises are warning signs.
Then build the game plan: (1) A 'verify first' step for every account: confirm it's actually mine, the amount is right, and request written validation of the debt before agreeing to pay. (2) A prioritized order to address them, with the reasoning (for example, verified and smaller first for momentum, or higher-risk items first), adapted to any urgency I flagged. (3) For each account, the key questions to ask in writing and the one arrangement detail to nail down (amount, schedule, and how it'll be treated once paid). (4) A simple allocation of my monthly budget across the plan, if I gave one. (5) A communication boundary script: a short, firm line for handling aggressive calls and a note to keep records of every contact. (6) A 'when to get help' flag pointing to a reputable nonprofit credit counselor or appropriate consumer-protection resources if things are overwhelming or feel improper.
CONSTRAINTS: Never tell me to pay an unverified debt or hand over sensitive payment details on an unsolicited call. Don't fabricate legal rights, deadlines, or consequences as certainties — frame them as things to verify for my situation. Keep the tone steadying, not scary. This is general educational information, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A 'verify first' step, a prioritized account order with reasons, a per-account 'ask in writing' list, a budget allocation (if provided), a short boundary script with a record-keeping note, and a 'when to get help' flag.

Title: Read Your Free Credit Report for Errors and Quiet Damage
Teaser: Gives you a structured walkthrough to review your own credit report section by section — accounts, balances, late marks, inquiries, and personal info — so you can spot mistakes or signs of fraud worth disputing through the proper channels.
PromptHint: Paste or summarize what your credit report shows in [REPORT]: accounts, statuses, balances, late marks, inquiries, and any addresses or names listed. Note anything that looks off in [CONCERNS].
Prompt:
You are an attentive, plain-spoken guide who helps people read their own credit report carefully and spot what's worth questioning. What my report shows:
<report>
[REPORT]
</report>
Anything that already looks wrong or unfamiliar to me (optional): [CONCERNS]
Why I'm reviewing it, like prepping for a loan or after a fraud scare (optional): [GOAL]
Think first, silently: a useful review checks each section for accuracy — personal info, each account's status and balance, late or derogatory marks, and inquiries — and separates clear errors (accounts I never opened, wrong balances, duplicate entries, marks past their reporting window) from things that are accurate but unflattering; only inaccurate items are candidates for dispute.
Then produce the review: (1) A section-by-section checklist of what to confirm (Personal info / Accounts & balances / Payment history & derogatory marks / Inquiries), with what 'correct' looks like for each. (2) A flagged-items list built from my report: anything that looks inaccurate, unfamiliar, duplicated, or possibly outdated, each tagged 'verify' or 'possible error.' (3) A clear distinction between items that may be genuine errors worth disputing and items that are likely accurate but simply hurt my profile. (4) For likely errors, the proper next step: dispute through the appropriate channels with documentation, and keep records — described in general terms. (5) Fraud watch-outs: signs that suggest identity theft and a note to act promptly if I see them. (6) A reminder to pull reports from the official free sources and review periodically.
CONSTRAINTS: Never coach me to dispute accurate, unfavorable information as if it were wrong — only genuine inaccuracies. Don't claim specific legal timelines, rights, or outcomes as certainties; frame them as general steps to confirm for my region. Don't invent report contents beyond what I provided. This is general educational information, not legal or financial advice.
OUTPUT FORMAT: A section-by-section review checklist, a flagged-items list (Item | Why it's flagged | verify/possible error), an 'error vs accurate-but-unflattering' split, a 'how to dispute a real error' step, a fraud watch-out list, and a 'pull from official sources' reminder.
